title_en: Key Themes: Contracts, Obligations, and Legal Principles
title_ar: المحاور الأساسية: العقود والالتزامات والقواعد الفقهية
content_en:
The 548 distinctions in Al-Furuq cover virtually every domain of Islamic positive law, but several thematic clusters stand out for their theoretical importance and practical significance. One of the most prominent is the treatment of contracts and transactions (mu'amalat). Al-Qarafi devotes extensive attention to the conditions that distinguish valid from invalid contracts, the difference between a contract's essential and accessory conditions, and the varying effects of defects in consent, offer and acceptance, and object specification. Many of the most instructive distinctions in the work concern cases where two transactions appear structurally similar but differ in a feature that, under Maliki principles, determines their validity.

Another significant cluster concerns obligations and liability. Al-Qarafi carefully distinguishes different types of duties — obligations toward God, obligations toward other humans, the duty to prevent harm versus the duty to confer benefit — and analyzes how these different types of obligation interact when they conflict. His treatment of liability for harm caused by others, for harm caused through one's property, and for harm caused through omission reflects a sophisticated understanding of the underlying principles of Maliki tort doctrine.

A theme of particular theoretical importance is al-Qarafi's treatment of the distinction between fatwa (legal opinion for individuals) and qada' (judicial ruling in court). He argues that these are not simply different institutional settings for applying the same rules, but are governed by different principles. A mufti issues an opinion based on what he believes is correct; a judge must apply the law as established, even if he personally disagrees. The mufti considers the private circumstances of the questioner; the judge must render a publicly enforceable ruling. This distinction — which al-Qarafi develops at length — has important implications for understanding the role of legal discretion in Islamic jurisprudence and has attracted significant attention from contemporary scholars of Islamic law.

Al-Qarafi also makes important contributions to the theory of custom ('urf) as a source of legal determination. He argues that many Maliki rulings implicitly depend on particular customary practices prevailing in specific times and places and that jurists must be alert to changes in custom that may require corresponding changes in the ruling. This proto-sociological insight was remarkably forward-looking and anticipates discussions in contemporary Islamic legal theory about the adaptation of Islamic law to changing social conditions.
